Browser's built-in tracking protection blocking the request
Corporate or parental control extension blocking the content

symptoms: Page loads but certain elements (ads, widgets, embedded content) are missing
DevTools Network tab shows specific requests with ERR_BLOCKED_BY_CLIENT status
Site functionality breaks because blocked resources are needed for features
Disabling the ad blocker for the site resolves the issue

fixSteps.title: Whitelist the site in your ad blocker
fixSteps.instruction: Click your ad blocker extension icon in the toolbar and add the current site to the whitelist or allowlist. This lets all requests through for that specific site.

fixSteps.title: Pause or disable the ad blocker temporarily
fixSteps.instruction: Click the ad blocker icon and select 'Pause' or 'Disable on this site'. Refresh the page to see if the blocked content loads.

fixSteps.title: Check other privacy extensions
fixSteps.instruction: If you have multiple privacy or security extensions, disable them one by one to identify which one is blocking the request. Check Privacy Badger, Ghostery, NoScript, etc.

fixSteps.title: Check browser built-in protection settings
fixSteps.instruction: In browser settings, look for tracking protection or privacy settings. In Edge, check 'Tracking prevention' levels. In Firefox, check 'Enhanced Tracking Protection'. Adjust or create an exception for the site.

whenToCallPro: This error rarely requires professional help. If it is caused by a corporate policy extension you cannot control, contact your IT department.

faq.q: Why does my ad blocker break some websites?
faq.a: Some websites rely on ad-related scripts for core functionality, not just ads. When the ad blocker blocks these scripts, it can inadvertently break login forms, video players, or navigation menus.

faq.q: Should I disable my ad blocker for all sites?
faq.a: No. Only whitelist specific sites that are not working correctly. Ad blockers provide important security and privacy benefits on most websites.

faq.q: Can I see exactly which requests are being blocked?
faq.a: Yes. Open DevTools (F12), go to the Network tab, and reload the page. Blocked requests will show ERR_BLOCKED_BY_CLIENT in the status column, along with the URL that was blocked.

title: Empty Response from Server
shortDescription: The server accepted the connection but sent back no data at all.
fullDescription: ERR_EMPTY_RESPONSE occurs when the browser successfully establishes a connection to the server, but the server closes the connection without sending any data back — no headers, no body, no HTTP status code. This is different from a connection refusal or timeout. The server literally responds with nothing. Common causes include server crashes during request processing, misconfigured server software, and firewall or security software silently dropping the response.

causes.cause: Server application crashed while processing the request
Firewall or antivirus stripping the response before it reaches the browser
Server misconfigured to close connections prematurely
Network equipment (proxy, load balancer) dropping the response

symptoms: Browser shows 'No data received' or 'This page isn't working' with ERR_EMPTY_RESPONSE
The page appears to start loading but the browser receives nothing
Error occurs consistently on specific pages or intermittently
Other websites load normally while the affected one returns empty

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache and cookies
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Privacy > Clear browsing data. Clear both cached files and cookies. Stale data can sometimes cause the server to respond incorrectly.

fixSteps.title: Disable browser extensions
fixSteps.instruction: Temporarily disable all extensions, especially security and privacy extensions that might intercept and strip responses. Test the site in incognito mode.

fixSteps.title: Flush DNS and reset network
fixSteps.instruction: Open Command Prompt as administrator and run: ipconfig /flushdns then netsh winsock reset. Restart your computer.

fixSteps.title: Temporarily disable antivirus web protection
fixSteps.instruction: Disable your antivirus web scanning or web shield feature temporarily. Some antivirus programs can strip or drop responses they cannot scan properly.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists across all browsers and you have ruled out local causes, the issue is server-side and only the website owner can fix it.

faq.q: What does 'no data received' mean?
faq.a: It means the server opened a connection with your browser but then sent absolutely nothing — no HTTP headers, no content, nothing. The connection was established but empty.

faq.q: Can a VPN cause ERR_EMPTY_RESPONSE?
faq.a: Yes. Some VPNs or proxy servers can interfere with certain responses, especially if they have content filtering enabled that cannot handle the response correctly and drops it.

faq.q: Is this error always the server's fault?
faq.a: Usually, but not always. Local software (antivirus, firewall, proxy) can strip or drop server responses before they reach the browser, making it appear as an empty response even though the server sent data.

title: Certificate Common Name Mismatch
shortDescription: The website's SSL certificate does not match the domain name in the address bar.
fullDescription:
ERR_CERT_COMMON_NAME_INVALID occurs when the SSL/TLS certificate presented by the server does not match the hostname the browser is trying to connect to. For example, a certificate issued for 'www.example.com' would trigger this error if you visit 'example.com' without 'www'.

This is a security warning that the site's identity cannot be verified. While it can be caused by simple misconfiguration on the server side, it can also indicate a man-in-the-middle attack where someone is intercepting your traffic with a fake certificate.

Modern browsers are strict about certificate name matching and will block access by default to protect users from potential security threats.

causes.cause: SSL certificate issued for a different domain name
Website recently changed domains but certificate was not updated
Antivirus software performing SSL inspection with its own certificate
Man-in-the-middle attack intercepting traffic

symptoms: Browser shows 'Your connection is not private' warning
Certificate error icon appears in the address bar
Error page mentions NET::ERR_CERT_COMMON_NAME_INVALID
Site works when accessed via a different URL variation

fixSteps.title: Check the URL spelling
fixSteps.instruction: Verify you typed the correct URL. Try adding or removing 'www.' from the beginning of the domain. Some certificates only cover one variation.

fixSteps.title: Check your system date and time
fixSteps.instruction: Incorrect system clock can cause certificate validation failures. Right-click the clock in the taskbar, select 'Adjust date/time', and enable 'Set time automatically'.

fixSteps.title: Disable antivirus SSL scanning
fixSteps.instruction: Open your antivirus settings and look for HTTPS scanning, SSL inspection, or encrypted connection scanning. Disable it temporarily and reload the page.

fixSteps.title: Clear SSL state
fixSteps.instruction: Open Internet Options (search in Start menu) > Content tab > Clear SSL State. Then restart your browser.

whenToCallPro: If you suspect a man-in-the-middle attack or the error appears on banking and financial sites, consult a security professional immediately.

faq.q: Is it safe to bypass ERR_CERT_COMMON_NAME_INVALID?
faq.a: Only if you trust the site and understand the risk. Never bypass this warning on sites where you enter passwords, financial information, or personal data.

faq.q: Why does this error appear on internal company sites?
faq.a: Internal sites often use self-signed certificates or certificates issued for internal domain names that do not match what the browser expects. Your IT department can install the proper root certificate.

title: SSL Certificate Has Been Revoked
shortDescription: The website's SSL certificate has been revoked by the issuing certificate authority.
fullDescription:
ERR_CERT_REVOKED appears when the browser detects that a website's SSL certificate has been explicitly revoked by the certificate authority (CA) that issued it. Certificate revocation typically occurs when a certificate's private key has been compromised, the certificate was issued fraudulently, or the domain ownership has changed.

This is a serious security warning. Unlike expired certificates, a revoked certificate indicates an active security concern. The certificate authority has determined that the certificate should no longer be trusted under any circumstances.

Browsers check certificate revocation status using CRL (Certificate Revocation Lists) or OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) to ensure certificates have not been revoked since issuance.

causes.cause: Certificate private key was compromised
Website owner requested revocation after migration
Certificate authority revoked due to policy violation
Cached revocation data is outdated or corrupted

symptoms: Browser displays 'Your connection is not private' with ERR_CERT_REVOKED
Cannot proceed to the website even with advanced options
Error appears suddenly on a previously working site
Other browsers also block the same site

fixSteps.title: Clear SSL state and browser cache
fixSteps.instruction: Open Internet Options > Content tab > Clear SSL State. Then clear browser cache via Settings > Privacy > Clear browsing data. Restart the browser.

fixSteps.title: Check system date and time
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure your system clock is accurate. An incorrect date can cause revocation check failures. Open Settings > Time & Language and enable automatic time sync.

fixSteps.title: Disable antivirus certificate checking
fixSteps.instruction: Some antivirus programs interfere with certificate revocation checks. Temporarily disable HTTPS/SSL scanning in your antivirus and test again.

fixSteps.title: Contact the website owner
fixSteps.instruction: If you are the site administrator, contact your certificate authority to investigate the revocation and obtain a new certificate. If you are a visitor, notify the site owner.

whenToCallPro: If you manage the affected website and need to obtain a new certificate or investigate a potential key compromise.

faq.q: Should I ever bypass ERR_CERT_REVOKED?
faq.a: No. A revoked certificate indicates a confirmed security issue. Unlike other certificate warnings, this one should never be bypassed as the certificate has been explicitly invalidated.

faq.q: Why was the certificate revoked?
faq.a: Common reasons include the site's private key being stolen or leaked, the certificate being issued to the wrong entity, or the site owner requesting revocation after switching to a new certificate.

title: Certificate Uses Weak Signature Algorithm
shortDescription: The website's SSL certificate uses an outdated and insecure signature algorithm.
fullDescription:
NET_ERR_CERT_WEAK_SIGNATURE occurs when a website's SSL certificate is signed using a cryptographic algorithm that modern browsers consider too weak to be secure, such as SHA-1 or MD5. These algorithms are vulnerable to collision attacks, meaning an attacker could potentially forge a certificate.

Major browsers began rejecting SHA-1 signed certificates in 2017 as the algorithm was proven to be practically breakable. Websites and certificate authorities have since migrated to SHA-256 and stronger algorithms.

This error is almost always a server-side issue that the website administrator needs to fix by obtaining a new certificate with a modern signature algorithm.

causes.cause: Certificate signed with deprecated SHA-1 algorithm
Intermediate certificate in the chain uses weak signature
Legacy server configuration not updated
Corporate proxy re-signing with weak algorithm

symptoms: Browser blocks the site with a certificate security warning
Error message mentions weak signature or insecure algorithm
Site may have worked in the past but stopped after browser update
Older browsers may still load the site without error

fixSteps.title: Verify the certificate details
fixSteps.instruction: Click the lock icon in the address bar (or the 'Not Secure' warning) and view the certificate details. Check the 'Signature Algorithm' field to confirm it uses SHA-1 or MD5.

fixSteps.title: Contact the website administrator
fixSteps.instruction: If you do not own the site, report the issue to the website administrator. They need to obtain a new certificate signed with SHA-256 or stronger.

fixSteps.title: Check for proxy interference
fixSteps.instruction: If you are on a corporate network, your proxy may be re-signing certificates with a weak algorithm. Contact your IT department to update the proxy's signing configuration.

fixSteps.title: Renew the certificate (site owners)
fixSteps.instruction: If you manage the site, request a new certificate from your CA specifying SHA-256 signing. Free CAs like Let's Encrypt use SHA-256 by default.

whenToCallPro: If you manage the website and are unsure how to replace the SSL certificate or configure the certificate chain properly.

faq.q: Can I force the browser to accept weak signature certificates?
faq.a: Some browsers allow bypassing the warning, but this is strongly discouraged. Weak signatures are genuinely insecure and could allow attackers to forge certificates.

faq.q: What signature algorithm should certificates use?
faq.a: Modern certificates should use SHA-256 (also called SHA-2) or stronger. All major certificate authorities now issue SHA-256 certificates by default.

title: Proxy Connection Failed
shortDescription: The browser was unable to establish a connection through the configured proxy server.
fullDescription:
ERR_PROXY_CONNECTION_FAILED occurs when the browser is configured to use a proxy server but cannot establish a connection to it. The proxy server may be down, unreachable, or the proxy settings may be incorrect.

Proxy servers act as intermediaries between your browser and the internet. When a proxy connection fails, no web traffic can pass through, effectively blocking all internet access through the browser even though your underlying network connection may be perfectly fine.

This error is common in corporate environments where proxy use is mandatory, as well as when users have manually configured proxy settings or when malware has altered proxy configurations without the user's knowledge.

causes.cause: Proxy server is down or unreachable
Incorrect proxy address or port configured
Malware changed proxy settings without user knowledge
VPN software conflicting with proxy configuration
PAC (Proxy Auto-Config) script is unreachable or malformed

symptoms: All websites fail to load with proxy connection error
Browser shows 'Unable to connect to the proxy server'
Other applications not using the proxy still have internet access
Error appears immediately without loading delay

fixSteps.title: Disable proxy settings
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Windows Settings > Network & Internet > Proxy. Turn off 'Use a proxy server' and 'Use setup script'. Also turn off 'Automatically detect settings' temporarily to test.

fixSteps.title: Check browser-specific proxy settings
fixSteps.instruction: In Chrome, go to Settings > System > Open proxy settings. In Firefox, go to Settings > General > Network Settings. Ensure proxy is set to 'No proxy' or 'Use system proxy settings'.

fixSteps.title: Scan for malware
fixSteps.instruction: Run a full system scan with Windows Security or your antivirus. Malware often changes proxy settings to route traffic through malicious servers.

fixSteps.title: Reset network settings
fixSteps.instruction: Open Command Prompt as administrator and run: netsh winhttp reset proxy. Then restart the browser.

whenToCallPro: If proxy settings keep reverting after you change them, malware may be involved and professional malware removal is recommended.

faq.q: Why do my proxy settings keep changing back?
faq.a: This is a strong indicator of malware. Run a full antivirus scan and consider using a dedicated malware removal tool like Malwarebytes to clean the infection.

faq.q: Do I need a proxy to browse the internet?
faq.a: Most home users do not need a proxy. If you did not deliberately configure one and are not on a corporate network, you can safely disable all proxy settings.

title: Proxy Tunnel Connection Failed
shortDescription: The browser failed to create a tunnel through the proxy server for HTTPS traffic.
fullDescription:
ERR_TUNNEL_CONNECTION_FAILED occurs when the browser cannot establish an HTTPS tunnel through a proxy server using the HTTP CONNECT method. This is specifically related to how HTTPS traffic is handled through proxies — the browser asks the proxy to create a secure tunnel to the destination, and the proxy either refuses or fails to do so.

Unlike ERR_PROXY_CONNECTION_FAILED which means the proxy itself is unreachable, this error means the proxy was reached but the tunnel creation failed. This can happen when the proxy does not support HTTPS tunneling, blocks certain destinations, or has authentication issues.

This error is most commonly seen in corporate or school networks where proxy servers may restrict access to certain HTTPS sites.

causes.cause: Proxy server blocking the destination website
Proxy requires authentication credentials
Proxy does not support CONNECT method for HTTPS
Incorrect proxy configuration for HTTPS traffic

symptoms: HTTPS sites fail but HTTP sites may work through the proxy
Error message mentions tunnel connection failure
Sites blocked by the proxy show this error instead of a block page
Error occurs only on specific websites

fixSteps.title: Try removing proxy settings
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Windows Settings > Network & Internet > Proxy. Disable the proxy temporarily. If sites load, the proxy is the issue.

fixSteps.title: Enter proxy authentication
fixSteps.instruction: If your proxy requires a username and password, the browser may prompt you. Enter your network credentials. If no prompt appears, check your proxy settings for authentication options.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser data and DNS cache
fixSteps.instruction: Clear all browsing data from the browser settings. Then open Command Prompt and run: ipconfig /flushdns to clear the DNS cache.

fixSteps.title: Contact network administrator
fixSteps.instruction: If you are on a corporate or school network, the proxy may be intentionally blocking the site. Contact your IT helpdesk to request access or get the correct proxy configuration.

whenToCallPro: If you are on a managed corporate network and cannot modify proxy settings yourself, your IT department needs to assist.

faq.q: What is the difference between ERR_PROXY_CONNECTION_FAILED and ERR_TUNNEL_CONNECTION_FAILED?
faq.a: ERR_PROXY_CONNECTION_FAILED means you cannot reach the proxy server at all. ERR_TUNNEL_CONNECTION_FAILED means you reached the proxy but it could not create a secure tunnel to the destination website.

faq.q: Can a VPN help bypass this error?
faq.a: Yes, a VPN bypasses the proxy entirely by creating its own encrypted tunnel. However, on managed networks this may violate usage policies.

title: Content Decoding Failed
shortDescription: The browser could not decode or decompress the response data from the server.
fullDescription:
ERR_CONTENT_DECODING_FAILED occurs when the server sends compressed content (usually gzip or Brotli encoded) but the browser is unable to decompress it. The server indicates the encoding type in the Content-Encoding HTTP header, and if the actual data does not match the declared encoding, the browser cannot process it.

This usually happens due to server misconfiguration where the Content-Encoding header says the response is compressed but the data is actually sent uncompressed, or vice versa. It can also occur when a proxy or CDN modifies the response body without updating the headers.

The issue is almost always server-side, but clearing browser cache can sometimes resolve it if the browser has cached a corrupted version of the response.

causes.cause: Server misconfigured Content-Encoding header
CDN or proxy corrupting compressed responses
Browser cache contains corrupted compressed data
Antivirus modifying response content without fixing headers

symptoms: Page fails to load with content decoding error
Error appears on specific pages but not the entire site
Issue may be intermittent depending on cache state
Same page loads fine in a different browser

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache
fixSteps.instruction: Open browser settings > Privacy > Clear browsing data. Select 'Cached images and files'. Restart the browser and reload the page.

fixSteps.title: Try incognito mode
fixSteps.instruction: Open an incognito/private window and try loading the page. This bypasses the cache and can confirm whether cached data is the problem.

fixSteps.title: Disable browser extensions
fixSteps.instruction: Some extensions modify response content. Disable all extensions temporarily and reload the page. If it works, re-enable extensions one by one to find the culprit.

fixSteps.title: Report to website administrator
fixSteps.instruction: If clearing cache does not help, the issue is server-side. Contact the website owner and report the ERR_CONTENT_DECODING_FAILED error so they can check their compression configuration.

whenToCallPro: If you manage the website server and need help configuring content encoding and compression headers properly.

faq.q: Why does this error only affect certain pages?
faq.a: Different pages may be served by different backend processes or cached differently at the CDN level. Only pages with misconfigured compression will trigger this error.

faq.q: Is this error dangerous?
faq.a: No. This is a technical content delivery issue, not a security threat. No data is at risk — the browser simply cannot read the garbled response.

title: Connection to Unsafe Port Blocked
shortDescription: The browser blocked the connection because it targets a port number that is restricted for security reasons.
fullDescription:
ERR_UNSAFE_PORT occurs when you try to access a website or service on a port number that the browser considers unsafe. Browsers maintain a blocklist of ports commonly used by non-HTTP services such as FTP (port 21), SMTP (port 25), and others. Allowing HTTP requests to these ports could enable cross-protocol attacks.

This security measure prevents websites from using your browser to send requests to local services running on your machine or network that were never meant to receive web traffic. For example, a malicious website could try to send commands to an email server on port 25.

The restricted ports list is defined by the browser vendor and cannot be modified by regular users. Common restricted ports include 21, 22, 23, 25, 110, 143, 993, and 995.

causes.cause: URL specifies a port on the browser's restricted list
Web application configured to run on a non-standard unsafe port
Development server accidentally bound to a restricted port
Redirect chain leading to a URL with a restricted port

symptoms: Browser shows ERR_UNSAFE_PORT immediately without attempting connection
Error occurs only when a specific port number is in the URL
Removing the port number from the URL resolves the error
Same URL works with curl or other non-browser tools

fixSteps.title: Check the port number
fixSteps.instruction: Look at the URL in the address bar. If it contains a port number like :25, :21, or :110, these are restricted. Try accessing the service on a standard web port like 80, 443, or 8080 instead.

fixSteps.title: Change the server port
fixSteps.instruction: If you control the server, reconfigure it to run on a non-restricted port. Common safe alternatives include 8080, 8443, 3000, or any port above 1024 not on the restricted list.

fixSteps.title: Use Chrome launch flag (advanced)
fixSteps.instruction: You can launch Chrome with --explicitly-allowed-ports=PORT to allow a specific port. Create a shortcut and add the flag. This is not recommended for regular browsing.

fixSteps.title: Use an alternative tool
fixSteps.instruction: If you need to access a non-HTTP service, use the appropriate client tool instead of the browser. For example, use an FTP client for port 21 or an email client for port 25.

whenToCallPro: If you need to configure a web server to run on a different port or set up port forwarding for development purposes.

faq.q: Why does the browser block certain ports?
faq.a: To prevent cross-protocol attacks where a malicious website could use your browser to send commands to non-HTTP services running on your computer or network.

faq.q: Which ports are blocked?
faq.a: Common blocked ports include 21 (FTP), 22 (SSH), 23 (Telnet), 25 (SMTP), 110 (POP3), 143 (IMAP), 993 (IMAPS), 995 (POP3S), and several others used by system services.

title: Request Blocked by Server Response Headers
shortDescription: The server's response headers instructed the browser to block the content from loading.
fullDescription:
ERR_BLOCKED_BY_RESPONSE occurs when the server sends response headers that instruct the browser to block the content. This is typically caused by Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) restrictions, X-Frame-Options headers preventing embedding, or Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy headers blocking cross-origin loading.

This error is a deliberate security mechanism. Website owners use these headers to prevent their content from being loaded or embedded by unauthorized third-party sites, protecting against clickjacking, data theft, and other cross-origin attacks.

As a regular user, this error usually means the website's security configuration is preventing a specific resource from loading in the current context. The website developer needs to adjust their server headers to allow the intended use case.

causes.cause: CORS policy blocking cross-origin resource requests
X-Frame-Options preventing page from being embedded in iframe
Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header blocking cross-origin loading
Content-Security-Policy blocking inline scripts or external resources

symptoms: Specific resources like images or scripts fail to load on a page
Embedded content from other sites shows blank or error
Browser console shows CORS or cross-origin errors
Page partially loads but some elements are missing

fixSteps.title: Check browser developer console
fixSteps.instruction: Press F12 to open Developer Tools and check the Console tab. The exact header causing the block will be mentioned in the error message.

fixSteps.title: Try disabling extensions
fixSteps.instruction: Some browser extensions add security headers or block cross-origin requests. Disable extensions and reload to check if an extension is the cause.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache
fixSteps.instruction: Cached responses with restrictive headers can persist. Clear the browser cache and reload the page.

fixSteps.title: Contact the website developer
fixSteps.instruction: If the issue is server-side CORS or header misconfiguration, report it to the website developer. They need to adjust their Access-Control-Allow-Origin, X-Frame-Options, or CORP headers.

whenToCallPro: If you are a website developer dealing with complex CORS configurations or Content-Security-Policy issues across multiple origins.

faq.q: What is CORS and why does it block content?
faq.a: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a security mechanism that restricts how web pages from one domain can request resources from another domain. It prevents malicious sites from stealing data from other sites using your browser.

faq.q: Can I disable CORS in my browser?
faq.a: While there are flags and extensions that can disable CORS checking, this is extremely dangerous for regular browsing and should only be used temporarily during development.

title: Local File Not Found
shortDescription: The browser could not find a local file that was referenced by a file:// URL.
fullDescription:
ERR_FILE_NOT_FOUND occurs when the browser tries to load a local file using a file:// URL scheme but the file does not exist at the specified path. This is different from HTTP 404, which refers to a file on a remote server.

This error commonly appears when a web page references a local resource that has been moved or deleted, when a browser extension tries to load a missing local file, or when you click a link to a local file that no longer exists.

The error can also occur with Chrome extensions that reference local files, saved HTML pages with broken local links, or when developer tools reference source maps from deleted projects.

causes.cause: Referenced local file was moved, renamed, or deleted
Browser extension referencing a missing local resource
Saved HTML page with broken relative file paths
Typo in the file:// URL path

symptoms: Browser shows ERR_FILE_NOT_FOUND with a file:// URL
A saved web page has missing images or stylesheets
Browser extension icons or popups appear broken
Developer tools show 404 errors for source map files

fixSteps.title: Verify the file path
fixSteps.instruction: Check the URL in the address bar. Ensure the file path is correct, including proper capitalization and file extension. Navigate to the folder in File Explorer to confirm the file exists.

fixSteps.title: Check for moved or renamed files
fixSteps.instruction: If the file was moved, update the URL or link to point to the new location. Use Windows Search to find the file if you are unsure where it was moved.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall or update problematic extension
fixSteps.instruction: If the error comes from a browser extension, go to Extensions settings, remove the extension, and reinstall it from the web store.

fixSteps.title: Re-save the web page
fixSteps.instruction: If you saved a web page and its local resources are broken, navigate to the original URL and save it again using 'Save page as' > 'Web Page, Complete'.

whenToCallPro: This error rarely requires professional help as it simply means a file is missing from your local system.

faq.q: Is ERR_FILE_NOT_FOUND the same as HTTP 404?
faq.a: No. ERR_FILE_NOT_FOUND refers to a missing file on your local computer accessed via file:// protocol. HTTP 404 refers to a missing resource on a remote web server.

faq.q: Why does my browser show this error when I did not open a local file?
faq.a: A browser extension or a web page may be trying to reference a local resource in the background. Check the developer console (F12) to see which resource triggered the error.

title: Request Aborted
shortDescription: The browser or server aborted the request before it completed.
fullDescription:
ERR_ABORTED occurs when a network request is intentionally cancelled before it completes. This can happen when the user navigates away from a page before it finishes loading, when a script cancels a pending request, or when the browser itself aborts the request due to policy restrictions.

Unlike connection errors where something goes wrong with the network, ERR_ABORTED is a deliberate cancellation. The request was in progress and was stopped either by user action, browser policy, or server-side logic.

This error is commonly seen in browser developer tools rather than as a full-page error message. It often appears in the Network tab for resources that were loading when the user navigated away, or for requests blocked by Content-Security-Policy.

causes.cause: User navigated away before page finished loading
Content-Security-Policy blocked a resource download
Browser extension cancelled the request
JavaScript code explicitly cancelled the fetch or XMLHttpRequest
Server closed connection before sending full response

symptoms: Resources fail to load and show ERR_ABORTED in developer tools
Page loads partially with missing scripts or styles
Download starts but immediately fails
Form submission appears to do nothing

fixSteps.title: Reload the page completely
fixSteps.instruction: Press Ctrl+Shift+R to do a hard reload that bypasses cache. Wait for the page to fully load without navigating away.

fixSteps.title: Check for blocking extensions
fixSteps.instruction: Ad blockers and security extensions commonly abort requests. Disable extensions one by one and reload to identify the culprit.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache
fixSteps.instruction: Corrupted cache entries can cause repeated aborted requests. Clear cached files from browser settings and reload.

fixSteps.title: Check developer console for details
fixSteps.instruction: Press F12, go to the Console and Network tabs. Look for Content-Security-Policy violations or specific error messages explaining why the request was aborted.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists on a site you manage and involves CSP policy or server configuration issues.

faq.q: Is ERR_ABORTED a serious error?
faq.a: Usually not. It often just means a request was cancelled because you navigated away. It becomes a concern only when requests are being aborted unexpectedly and repeatedly.

faq.q: Why do I see many ERR_ABORTED errors in the Network tab?
faq.a: If you navigate away from a page quickly, all pending requests for that page get aborted. This is normal browser behavior and not a sign of a problem.

title: Insufficient System Resources
shortDescription: The browser ran out of system resources such as memory or network connections to complete the request.
fullDescription:
ERR_INSUFFICIENT_RESOURCES occurs when the browser cannot allocate the system resources needed to process a request. This typically means the system is running low on RAM, the browser has too many tabs open consuming memory, or the maximum number of network connections has been reached.

Modern browsers are resource-intensive applications, and each tab consumes a significant amount of memory. When the system runs out of available memory or the browser reaches its internal resource limits, new requests cannot be processed and this error appears.

This error is more common on systems with limited RAM (4 GB or less) or when running many applications simultaneously alongside a browser with numerous open tabs.

causes.cause: Too many browser tabs open consuming all available memory
System running low on RAM due to other applications
Memory leak in a browser extension or web page
Browser process limit reached
Low disk space affecting virtual memory

symptoms: New tabs fail to load with resource error
Browser becomes slow and unresponsive before the error
Existing tabs may crash or show 'Aw, Snap!' errors
System may show low memory warnings

fixSteps.title: Close unnecessary tabs
fixSteps.instruction: Close tabs you are not actively using. In Chrome, right-click a tab and select 'Close other tabs' to keep only the current one. Each tab uses 50-300 MB of RAM.

fixSteps.title: Check system resource usage
fixSteps.instruction: Press Ctrl+Shift+Esc to open Task Manager. Check the Memory column to see which applications are using the most RAM. Close unnecessary applications.

fixSteps.title: Use browser task manager
fixSteps.instruction: In Chrome or Edge, press Shift+Esc to open the browser's own task manager. Identify tabs or extensions using excessive memory and close them.

fixSteps.title: Restart the browser
fixSteps.instruction: Close the browser completely and reopen it. This releases all accumulated memory. Consider using a session manager extension to save tabs for later rather than keeping them open.

fixSteps.title: Increase virtual memory
fixSteps.instruction: If you frequently run out of memory, increase Windows virtual memory. Go to System Properties > Advanced > Performance Settings > Advanced > Virtual Memory > Change. Set a larger custom size.

whenToCallPro: If the error occurs with minimal tabs open and sufficient RAM, there may be a deeper system issue or memory leak requiring investigation.

faq.q: How much RAM do I need for comfortable browsing?
faq.a: 8 GB is the minimum recommended for modern browsing. If you regularly have 20+ tabs open, 16 GB is recommended. Chrome is particularly memory-intensive.

faq.q: Do browser extensions use resources?
faq.a: Yes, each extension runs as a separate process and consumes memory. Extensions with background scripts consume resources even when you are not actively using them. Audit and remove unused extensions regularly.

title: DNS Probe Possible Issue Detected
shortDescription: The browser detected a possible DNS issue that may be preventing the page from loading.
fullDescription:
DNS_PROBE_POSSIBLE is a transitional DNS error that appears when the browser suspects a DNS resolution issue but has not yet fully determined the cause. It is less definitive than DNS_PROBE_FINISHED_NXDOMAIN or DNS_PROBE_FINISHED_NO_INTERNET and often resolves itself quickly.

This error typically appears briefly before the browser determines the actual DNS status. If it persists, it usually indicates an intermittent DNS issue where the DNS server is slow to respond or is experiencing temporary difficulties.

Unlike the more specific DNS errors, DNS_PROBE_POSSIBLE suggests the browser is still attempting to diagnose the problem and may succeed on a retry.

causes.cause: DNS server temporarily slow or unresponsive
Intermittent network connectivity
DNS cache containing stale or corrupted entries
DNS server overloaded with requests

symptoms: Page loading stalls and shows DNS_PROBE_POSSIBLE
Error may resolve itself after refreshing
Some websites load while others do not
Error is intermittent and not consistent

fixSteps.title: Refresh the page
fixSteps.instruction: Simply press F5 or click the refresh button. DNS_PROBE_POSSIBLE often resolves on retry as the DNS query completes on the second attempt.

fixSteps.title: Flush DNS cache
fixSteps.instruction: Open Command Prompt as administrator and run: ipconfig /flushdns. This clears any stale or corrupted DNS entries.

fixSteps.title: Switch to a public DNS server
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Control Panel > Network and Sharing Center > Change adapter settings. Right-click your connection > Properties > IPv4 > Properties. Set DNS to 8.8.8.8 and 8.8.4.4 (Google DNS).

fixSteps.title: Restart DNS Client service
fixSteps.instruction: Open Command Prompt as administrator and run: net stop dnscache then net start dnscache. This restarts the Windows DNS resolver.

whenToCallPro: If DNS errors persist across all sites after changing DNS servers, there may be a deeper network configuration issue.

faq.q: How is DNS_PROBE_POSSIBLE different from DNS_PROBE_FINISHED_NXDOMAIN?
faq.a: DNS_PROBE_POSSIBLE means the browser suspects a DNS issue but is not certain. DNS_PROBE_FINISHED_NXDOMAIN means the DNS server definitively reported that the domain does not exist.

faq.q: Should I be worried about this error?
faq.a: Usually not. DNS_PROBE_POSSIBLE is often a temporary glitch that resolves itself. If it happens frequently, switching to a reliable public DNS server like Google DNS or Cloudflare DNS typically fixes it.

title: Network I/O Suspended
shortDescription: Network input/output operations have been suspended, typically due to system sleep or hibernate.
fullDescription:
ERR_NETWORK_IO_SUSPENDED occurs when the browser's network operations are suspended by the operating system. This typically happens when a computer wakes from sleep or hibernate and the network stack has not fully reinitialized yet.

When Windows enters sleep or hibernate mode, all network connections are closed and the network hardware is powered down. When the system wakes up, it takes a moment for the network adapter to reconnect, the IP address to be reassigned, and DNS resolution to begin working again. During this transition period, any browser requests will fail with this error.

The error is almost always temporary and resolves itself within seconds to a minute as the network reconnects.

causes.cause: Computer just woke from sleep or hibernate
Network adapter temporarily disconnected during driver update
Power management settings aggressively disabling network adapter
System running in low-power mode restricting network activity

symptoms: All pages fail to load after waking computer from sleep
Error resolves after waiting 10-30 seconds
Wi-Fi icon shows connected but pages do not load
Error accompanied by brief 'No internet' notification

fixSteps.title: Wait and refresh
fixSteps.instruction: Wait 15-30 seconds for the network to fully reconnect after waking from sleep, then press F5 to refresh the page.

fixSteps.title: Toggle airplane mode
fixSteps.instruction: If waiting does not help, toggle Airplane mode on and off from the system tray network icon. This forces a full network reconnection.

fixSteps.title: Disable network adapter power management
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager > Network adapters > right-click your adapter > Properties > Power Management tab. Uncheck 'Allow the computer to turn off this device to save power'.

fixSteps.title: Reset network adapter
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Windows Settings > Network & Internet > Advanced network settings > Network reset. This reinstalls network adapters and resets components. Your computer will restart.

whenToCallPro: If network I/O suspension persists long after waking and a network reset does not help, the network adapter driver may need to be updated or the hardware may be failing.

faq.q: Why does this happen every time my computer wakes from sleep?
faq.a: Your network adapter's power management settings may be too aggressive. Disabling the option to power off the adapter during sleep can prevent this issue.

faq.q: Does this error mean my Wi-Fi is broken?
faq.a: No. It is a temporary state while the network hardware reinitializes after sleep. The error should resolve within seconds to a minute.

title: Network Authentication Required
shortDescription: The network requires authentication before granting internet access, typically on captive portal Wi-Fi networks.
fullDescription:
HTTP 511 Network Authentication Required indicates that the client needs to authenticate to gain network access. This status code is most commonly encountered when connecting to public Wi-Fi networks that use a captive portal — a login page that intercepts web traffic until the user agrees to terms or enters credentials.

This is different from HTTP 401 (which requires authentication with a specific website). HTTP 511 means the network itself requires authentication before any web traffic can pass through.

Hotels, airports, coffee shops, and other public venues commonly use captive portals. The HTTP 511 status helps browsers distinguish between a website being down and the network requiring login.

causes.cause: Connected to a public Wi-Fi with a captive portal login page
Hotel or venue Wi-Fi session has expired and needs re-authentication
Corporate network requires VPN or portal authentication
ISP requiring authentication for internet access

symptoms: All websites redirect to a login or terms-of-service page
Wi-Fi shows connected but no internet access
Browser shows a captive portal login page instead of the requested website
Secure HTTPS sites fail while the captive portal redirect may work on HTTP

fixSteps.title: Open the captive portal login
fixSteps.instruction: Try navigating to any HTTP (not HTTPS) website like http://neverssl.com or http://example.com. The captive portal should redirect you to the login page. Complete the login or accept terms.

fixSteps.title: Use the network login notification
fixSteps.instruction: Windows usually displays a 'Sign in to network' notification when a captive portal is detected. Click it to open the login page directly.

fixSteps.title: Forget and reconnect to Wi-Fi
fixSteps.instruction: If the portal does not appear, go to Wi-Fi settings, click your network > Forget. Then reconnect. The captive portal should trigger again.

fixSteps.title: Try a different browser
fixSteps.instruction: Some captive portals work poorly with certain browsers. Try opening the login page in a different browser like Edge if Chrome is not working.

whenToCallPro: If you cannot access the captive portal login page at all, ask the venue staff for help or alternative connection instructions.

faq.q: Why do HTTPS sites not work on public Wi-Fi before login?
faq.a: Captive portals work by intercepting HTTP traffic and redirecting to a login page. HTTPS traffic cannot be intercepted this way due to encryption, so it simply fails instead of redirecting.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public Wi-Fi after logging in?
faq.a: Public Wi-Fi is inherently less secure. Use HTTPS websites, avoid entering sensitive information, and consider using a VPN for additional protection.

title: Firefox: Expired SSL Certificate
shortDescription: Firefox reports that the website's SSL certificate has passed its expiration date.
fullDescription:
SEC_ERROR_EXPIRED_CERTIFICATE is a Firefox-specific error that occurs when the website's SSL/TLS certificate has passed its validity end date. SSL certificates are issued with a fixed validity period, and once that period expires, browsers will no longer trust the certificate.

This error can occur for two reasons: the website's certificate has genuinely expired and the administrator has not renewed it, or your computer's system clock is set to an incorrect date, making a valid certificate appear expired.

Firefox displays this error with its own error code format (SEC_ERROR_*) while Chrome and Edge use NET::ERR_CERT_DATE_INVALID for the same issue. The underlying problem and solutions are identical across browsers.

causes.cause: Website SSL certificate has genuinely expired
System clock is set to a future date making the certificate appear expired
CMOS battery dead causing system clock to reset
Antivirus software presenting its own expired certificate

symptoms: Firefox shows 'Warning: Potential Security Risk Ahead'
Error code SEC_ERROR_EXPIRED_CERTIFICATE appears in details
Cannot access the site without accepting the risk
Same site works in other browsers or shows a different certificate error

fixSteps.title: Check your system date and time
fixSteps.instruction: Right-click the clock in the Windows taskbar and select 'Adjust date/time'. Ensure the date is correct and 'Set time automatically' is enabled. Sync the time by clicking 'Sync now'.

fixSteps.title: View the certificate expiration date
fixSteps.instruction: On the error page, click 'Advanced' then 'View Certificate'. Check the 'Not After' date to see when the certificate expired. If it expired recently, the site owner likely needs to renew.

fixSteps.title: Clear Firefox certificate cache
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Firefox Settings > Privacy & Security > Certificates > View Certificates > Authorities tab. Delete any unusual or expired entries. Also try clearing site data.

fixSteps.title: Temporarily disable antivirus SSL scanning
fixSteps.instruction: Some antivirus programs use their own certificates to inspect HTTPS traffic. If their certificate expires, all sites show this error. Disable HTTPS scanning and test.

whenToCallPro: If the error appears on all HTTPS websites and your system clock is correct, the issue may be with your antivirus or a corrupted certificate store requiring professional attention.

faq.q: Is it safe to bypass SEC_ERROR_EXPIRED_CERTIFICATE?
faq.a: If the certificate only recently expired and you trust the website, it is relatively low risk. However, do not enter sensitive information on sites with expired certificates.

faq.q: Why does Firefox show a different error code than Chrome?
faq.a: Firefox uses its own NSS (Network Security Services) library with its own error codes (SEC_ERROR_*), while Chrome uses BoringSSL with NET::ERR_* codes. The underlying issues are the same.

title: Firefox: Unknown Certificate Issuer
shortDescription: Firefox does not recognize the certificate authority that issued the website's SSL certificate.
fullDescription:
SEC_ERROR_UNKNOWN_ISSUER is a Firefox-specific error indicating that the browser does not trust the certificate authority (CA) that signed the website's SSL certificate. Firefox maintains its own certificate store separate from Windows, so this error can appear in Firefox while Chrome and Edge work fine using the Windows certificate store.

This commonly occurs when a website uses a certificate from a CA that Firefox does not include in its trusted root store, when a corporate environment uses a private CA for internal sites, or when an antivirus program intercepts HTTPS traffic using its own untrusted certificate.

The most frequent cause of this error in everyday use is antivirus software performing HTTPS/SSL inspection by replacing the site's certificate with its own. If the antivirus root certificate is not installed in Firefox's certificate store, this error appears on every HTTPS site.

causes.cause: Antivirus SSL inspection replacing certificates with untrusted ones
Corporate network using private certificate authority
Website using self-signed certificate
Missing intermediate certificate in server's certificate chain
Corrupted Firefox certificate database

symptoms: Firefox shows 'Warning: Potential Security Risk Ahead'
Error details show SEC_ERROR_UNKNOWN_ISSUER
Same site works fine in Chrome or Edge
Error appears on many or all HTTPS websites

fixSteps.title: Check if antivirus is causing the issue
fixSteps.instruction: Temporarily disable HTTPS/SSL scanning in your antivirus. If the error goes away, you need to import your antivirus root certificate into Firefox: Settings > Privacy & Security > Certificates > View Certificates > Import.

fixSteps.title: Enable Windows certificate store in Firefox
fixSteps.instruction: Type about:config in Firefox address bar, accept the risk, search for security.enterprise_roots.enabled and set it to true. This makes Firefox trust certificates from the Windows certificate store.

fixSteps.title: Delete and rebuild certificate database
fixSteps.instruction: Close Firefox. Navigate to your Firefox profile folder (type about:support in the address bar, click 'Open Folder'). Delete the file 'cert9.db'. Restart Firefox — it will create a fresh database.

fixSteps.title: Import corporate certificate manually
fixSteps.instruction: If on a corporate network, get the root CA certificate from your IT department. In Firefox, go to Settings > Privacy & Security > Certificates > View Certificates > Authorities > Import.

whenToCallPro: If the error appears on all HTTPS sites and you cannot identify the cause, or if you need help importing enterprise certificates.

faq.q: Why does this error only appear in Firefox?
faq.a: Firefox uses its own certificate store, separate from Windows. Chrome and Edge use the Windows store. If a certificate is trusted by Windows but not by Firefox, only Firefox shows this error.

faq.q: Is it safe to add an exception for this error?
faq.a: Only for sites you fully trust, like internal company sites with known self-signed certificates. Never add exceptions for public websites you do not control.

title: SSL Bad Record MAC Alert
shortDescription: The SSL/TLS connection was rejected because a message authentication code check failed.
fullDescription:
ERR_SSL_BAD_RECORD_MAC_ALERT occurs when the browser receives an SSL/TLS record with an invalid Message Authentication Code (MAC). The MAC is a cryptographic check that ensures data was not tampered with during transmission. When this check fails, the browser terminates the connection as a security precaution.

This error can indicate that data was corrupted during network transmission, a middlebox (like a firewall or proxy) is incorrectly modifying encrypted traffic, or there is a hardware issue with the network adapter causing bit errors.

In rare cases, this error can indicate a man-in-the-middle attack where someone is attempting to modify encrypted data. However, network hardware issues and misconfigured middleboxes are far more common causes.

causes.cause: Network hardware causing packet corruption
Firewall or proxy incorrectly modifying encrypted traffic
Faulty network adapter or driver
Wi-Fi interference causing data corruption
Man-in-the-middle attack tampering with data

symptoms: HTTPS sites fail intermittently with this error
Error is more frequent on certain networks
HTTP sites may work fine while HTTPS fails
Problem may go away when switching from Wi-Fi to wired connection

fixSteps.title: Switch to wired connection
fixSteps.instruction: If on Wi-Fi, connect via Ethernet cable. Wi-Fi interference can corrupt packets, triggering MAC validation failures.

fixSteps.title: Update network adapter driver
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager > Network adapters > right-click your adapter > Update driver > Search automatically. A buggy driver can cause packet corruption.

fixSteps.title: Disable hardware offloading
fixSteps.instruction: In Device Manager > Network adapters > right-click adapter > Properties > Advanced tab. Disable 'Large Send Offload', 'TCP Checksum Offload', and 'UDP Checksum Offload'.

fixSteps.title: Try a different network
fixSteps.instruction: Connect to a different network (mobile hotspot, different Wi-Fi) to determine if the issue is with your network or your computer.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists on all networks and after driver updates, the network adapter may need replacement or there may be a security concern.

faq.q: Is ERR_SSL_BAD_RECORD_MAC_ALERT a sign of hacking?
faq.a: While it can theoretically indicate tampering, the vast majority of cases are caused by network hardware issues or packet corruption. It is much more likely a technical problem than a security attack.

faq.q: Why does this only happen on some websites?
faq.a: Different websites may use different TLS configurations and cipher suites. Some may be more sensitive to minor packet corruption than others.

title: SSL Certificate Pinning Violation
shortDescription: The website's certificate chain does not include a public key that matches the site's pinned key expectations.
fullDescription:
ERR_SSL_PINNED_KEY_NOT_IN_CERT_CHAIN occurs when a website uses HTTP Public Key Pinning (HPKP) or built-in key pinning, and the certificate presented by the server does not include any of the expected public keys in its chain. This is a strict security check that prevents even valid certificates from being accepted if they do not match known good keys.

This error is most commonly triggered when antivirus software or corporate proxies intercept HTTPS traffic and replace the website's certificate with their own. Since the replacement certificate uses different keys, the pinning check fails.

While HPKP headers have been deprecated by most browsers, Chrome still maintains a built-in pin set for Google domains and some other high-security sites. This error is therefore most commonly seen on Google services when HTTPS inspection is in use.

causes.cause: Antivirus HTTPS inspection replacing pinned certificates
Corporate proxy performing SSL inspection on pinned sites
Malware intercepting HTTPS traffic
Misconfigured CDN serving wrong certificate

symptoms: Google sites fail to load while other sites work
Error specifically mentions pinned key not in certificate chain
Cannot bypass the error even by clicking 'Advanced'
Other browsers without pinning may work or show different warnings

fixSteps.title: Disable antivirus HTTPS scanning
fixSteps.instruction: Open your antivirus settings and disable HTTPS scanning, SSL inspection, or encrypted connection scanning. This is the most common cause of this error.

fixSteps.title: Check for proxy interference
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Windows Settings > Network & Internet > Proxy. Ensure no proxy is configured that might be intercepting HTTPS. Also check browser proxy settings.

fixSteps.title: Run malware scan
fixSteps.instruction: Run a full scan with Windows Security and a second-opinion scanner like Malwarebytes. Malware that performs HTTPS interception will trigger pinning failures.

fixSteps.title: Contact IT department
fixSteps.instruction: If on a corporate network, the SSL inspection appliance may need to be configured to bypass certificate-pinned sites like Google. Contact IT to whitelist pinned domains.

whenToCallPro: If disabling antivirus and proxy does not help, and malware scans are clean, there may be a rootkit or deep system compromise that requires professional investigation.

faq.q: Why can I not bypass this error like other certificate warnings?
faq.a: Certificate pinning errors cannot be bypassed because they indicate a high-confidence security violation. The site has specifically declared which keys it trusts, and the presented certificate does not match.

faq.q: What is certificate pinning?
faq.a: Certificate pinning is a security mechanism where a website declares exactly which certificate public keys are allowed to authenticate it. This prevents attackers from using fraudulently obtained certificates, even from trusted CAs.

title: Server Address Unreachable
shortDescription: The browser resolved the domain name but cannot reach the server's IP address.
fullDescription:
ERR_ADDRESS_UNREACHABLE occurs when DNS resolution succeeds and the browser obtains the server's IP address, but the network path to that address is blocked or unreachable. The domain name is valid and exists, but the packets cannot reach their destination.

This differs from DNS errors where the domain cannot be resolved. In this case, the browser knows where to connect but the route is blocked — either by a firewall, network configuration issue, or because the server is on a network that is not accessible from your location.

Common scenarios include trying to access a server on a private IP range from outside the network, server firewall blocking your IP or region, or network routing issues between your ISP and the server.

causes.cause: Server is offline or its network is down
Firewall blocking outbound connections to the IP
DNS pointing to an unreachable private IP address
Routing issues between your ISP and the destination
VPN not connected to the right network to reach the server

symptoms: Browser shows 'This site can't be reached' with ERR_ADDRESS_UNREACHABLE
Ping to the server IP times out
tracert shows packets stopping at a specific hop
Site may be reachable from other networks

fixSteps.title: Check if the site is globally down
fixSteps.instruction: Use a service like isitdown.site or downdetector.com from another device to check if the server is accessible from other locations.

fixSteps.title: Flush DNS and try again
fixSteps.instruction: Open Command Prompt as administrator, run: ipconfig /flushdns. The DNS might be pointing to an old IP. Retry loading the page.

fixSteps.title: Try connecting through a VPN
fixSteps.instruction: If the server might be blocking your region or IP, use a VPN to connect from a different location. This can bypass routing issues and IP blocks.

fixSteps.title: Check firewall rules
fixSteps.instruction: Open Windows Security > Firewall & network protection > Advanced settings. Check outbound rules for any rules blocking the IP address or port you are trying to reach.

whenToCallPro: If routing issues persist and traceroute shows packets being dropped at your ISP's hop, contact your ISP for assistance.

faq.q: How is ERR_ADDRESS_UNREACHABLE different from ERR_CONNECTION_TIMED_OUT?
faq.a: ERR_ADDRESS_UNREACHABLE means the network path to the server is broken or blocked, and the browser knows quickly. ERR_CONNECTION_TIMED_OUT means the browser waited the full timeout period hoping for a response.

faq.q: Can my ISP cause this error?
faq.a: Yes. If your ISP has routing issues to the server's network, or if they are blocking certain IP addresses or ports, you can see this error even though the server is up and running.

title: Connection Closed Unexpectedly
shortDescription: The server closed the connection without sending a complete response.
fullDescription:
ERR_CONNECTION_CLOSED occurs when the server terminates the TCP connection without sending a proper HTTP response. Unlike ERR_CONNECTION_RESET which is an abrupt forced closure, ERR_CONNECTION_CLOSED is a graceful but premature closure — the server sends a FIN packet to close the connection before sending any response data.

This can happen when the server encounters an internal error and closes the connection rather than sending an error page, when load balancers terminate idle connections, or when the server's connection pool is exhausted.

The error is similar to ERR_EMPTY_RESPONSE but more specific — the connection was properly closed rather than just sending no data. This distinction can help server administrators diagnose the root cause.

causes.cause: Server application crashed while processing the request
Load balancer closing connections under heavy traffic
Server connection pool exhausted
Firewall or security appliance terminating the connection
Server-side timeout configuration too aggressive

symptoms: Page fails to load and shows connection closed error
Error is intermittent and may succeed on retry
Error is more frequent during peak traffic times
Other users report similar issues simultaneously

fixSteps.title: Refresh the page
fixSteps.instruction: Press F5 or Ctrl+R to reload. Since this error is often caused by momentary server issues, a simple retry frequently works.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser state
fixSteps.instruction: Clear cookies and cache for the specific site. Right-click the lock icon in the address bar > Site settings > Clear data.

fixSteps.title: Disable browser extensions
fixSteps.instruction: Extensions that modify requests (ad blockers, privacy tools) can cause servers to close connections. Disable them temporarily and test.

fixSteps.title: Try a different network
fixSteps.instruction: Switch to a different network (mobile hotspot, VPN) to rule out network-level interference from your ISP or local firewall.

whenToCallPro: If you manage the server and connections are being dropped consistently, server logs need to be analyzed to find the root cause.

faq.q: What is the difference between ERR_CONNECTION_CLOSED and ERR_CONNECTION_RESET?
faq.a: ERR_CONNECTION_CLOSED means the server gracefully closed the connection (sent FIN). ERR_CONNECTION_RESET means the connection was forcefully terminated (RST packet), which is more abrupt and often indicates a different type of problem.

faq.q: Why does this error only happen on one website?
faq.a: The issue is almost certainly server-side. That specific server may be under heavy load, experiencing crashes, or have a misconfigured connection timeout.

title: Invalid URL Format
shortDescription: The URL entered in the address bar is malformed and cannot be parsed by the browser.
fullDescription:
ERR_INVALID_URL occurs when the browser cannot parse the URL because it is malformed, contains invalid characters, or does not follow the standard URL format. URLs must follow specific syntax rules including a valid scheme (http://, https://), a valid domain or IP address, and properly encoded special characters.

Common causes include extra spaces in the URL, unsupported characters, double slashes in the wrong places, or missing scheme prefixes. This can also happen when JavaScript generates a malformed URL programmatically or when copying URLs from documents that add formatting characters.

This error is different from ERR_NAME_NOT_RESOLVED, which occurs when the URL format is valid but the domain does not exist. ERR_INVALID_URL means the URL itself is structurally broken and cannot even be sent as a request.

causes.cause: URL contains invalid or unsupported characters
URL was copy-pasted with hidden formatting characters
Missing or incorrect URL scheme (http:// or https://)
JavaScript generating a malformed URL

symptoms: Browser immediately shows ERR_INVALID_URL without trying to connect
URL in the address bar looks unusual or contains strange characters
Clicking a link leads to this error
URL works when typed manually but not when copy-pasted

fixSteps.title: Check the URL for errors
fixSteps.instruction: Look carefully at the URL in the address bar. Check for extra spaces, unusual characters, double slashes, or missing 'http://' or 'https://' at the beginning.

fixSteps.title: Type the URL manually
fixSteps.instruction: Instead of copy-pasting, manually type the URL in the address bar. This eliminates hidden characters that may have been included when copying from a document or email.

fixSteps.title: URL encode special characters
fixSteps.instruction: If the URL contains special characters like spaces or non-ASCII characters, they need to be URL-encoded. Spaces should be %20, for example. Use an online URL encoder if needed.

fixSteps.title: Search for the page instead
fixSteps.instruction: If you cannot fix the URL, try searching for the page title or content on Google or another search engine to find the correct URL.

whenToCallPro: This error rarely requires professional assistance as it is simply a URL formatting issue.

faq.q: What makes a URL invalid?
faq.a: A URL is invalid if it contains unencoded special characters (like spaces), has a missing or malformed scheme (http/https), uses invalid port numbers, or has structural issues like missing domain names.

faq.q: Why does a copy-pasted URL show this error?
faq.a: Copying from Word documents, PDFs, or rich-text emails can include hidden formatting characters like zero-width spaces, smart quotes, or line breaks that break the URL.

title: HTTP/2 Inadequate Transport Security
shortDescription: The server supports HTTP/2 but its TLS configuration does not meet the security requirements for HTTP/2.
fullDescription:
ERR_HTTP2_INADEQUATE_TRANSPORT_SECURITY occurs when a server negotiates HTTP/2 but its TLS configuration does not meet the minimum security requirements defined in the HTTP/2 specification (RFC 7540). HTTP/2 has stricter TLS requirements than HTTP/1.1, including specific cipher suite requirements and a minimum TLS version.

HTTP/2 requires TLS 1.2 or later and prohibits certain cipher suites that are considered weak. If a server offers HTTP/2 but uses a blacklisted cipher suite or an outdated TLS version, the browser will reject the connection with this error.

This is almost always a server-side configuration issue. The server administrator needs to update the TLS configuration to use cipher suites compatible with HTTP/2 or disable HTTP/2 until the TLS setup can be upgraded.

causes.cause: Server using TLS cipher suite blacklisted for HTTP/2
Server configured with TLS 1.0 or 1.1 alongside HTTP/2
Reverse proxy or load balancer with outdated TLS settings
CDN serving HTTP/2 with legacy cipher configuration

symptoms: Specific websites fail with transport security error
Error mentions HTTP/2 or h2 protocol
Site may work in Firefox but fail in Chrome
Error may appear after server or CDN configuration changes

fixSteps.title: Disable HTTP/2 in Chrome (temporary workaround)
fixSteps.instruction: Open chrome://flags, search for 'HTTP/2', and disable the HTTP/2 flag. This forces HTTP/1.1 which has looser TLS requirements. Restart Chrome.

fixSteps.title: Try a different browser
fixSteps.instruction: Firefox has slightly different HTTP/2 cipher requirements. Try loading the site in Firefox to see if it works.

fixSteps.title: Contact the website administrator
fixSteps.instruction: Report the error to the website owner. They need to update their TLS cipher suite configuration to be compatible with HTTP/2.

fixSteps.title: Update server TLS configuration (site owners)
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the server uses TLS 1.2+ with HTTP/2-compatible cipher suites. Recommended: TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 and TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384.

whenToCallPro: If you manage the server and need help configuring TLS cipher suites for HTTP/2 compatibility.

faq.q: What cipher suites does HTTP/2 require?
faq.a: HTTP/2 requires TLS 1.2+ and prohibits cipher suites using CBC mode, RC4, and other weak algorithms. GCM-based AEAD cipher suites like AES-128-GCM are recommended.

faq.q: Is it safe to disable HTTP/2 as a workaround?
faq.a: Disabling HTTP/2 is safe from a security perspective. You lose the performance benefits of HTTP/2 (multiplexing, header compression) but HTTP/1.1 still works securely over TLS.

title: SSL Client Certificate Required
shortDescription: The server requires a client SSL certificate for authentication but none was provided.
fullDescription:
ERR_SSL_CLIENT_AUTH_CERT_NEEDED occurs when a server requires mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication, meaning the client must present its own SSL certificate to prove identity. Unlike typical HTTPS where only the server presents a certificate, mTLS requires both sides to authenticate.

This authentication method is commonly used by government websites, financial institutions, corporate intranets, and VPN portals. Client certificates are typically stored on smart cards, USB tokens, or in the operating system's certificate store.

If you should have a client certificate but the browser is not presenting it, the certificate may not be installed correctly, may be expired, or the browser may not have access to the certificate store where it is located.

causes.cause: Client certificate not installed on the system
Client certificate has expired
Smart card or USB token not connected
Browser cannot access the certificate store
Wrong certificate being presented to the server

symptoms: Browser shows a certificate selection dialog with no certificates listed
Site immediately rejects connection with client auth error
Error occurs on sites that previously required smart card login
Site works on another machine where a client certificate is installed

fixSteps.title: Check if you have a client certificate
fixSteps.instruction: Open Internet Options > Content tab > Certificates > Personal tab. Check if a valid (not expired) client certificate is listed. If none are listed, you need to obtain one.

fixSteps.title: Connect smart card or token
fixSteps.instruction: If your certificate is on a smart card or USB token, ensure it is properly connected. Install any required smart card drivers or middleware software.

fixSteps.title: Import the client certificate
fixSteps.instruction: If you have a certificate file (.pfx or .p12), double-click it and follow the Certificate Import Wizard. Choose 'Personal' store and mark the key as exportable if needed.

fixSteps.title: Contact your IT department or certificate issuer
fixSteps.instruction: If you do not have a client certificate, contact the organization that manages the website. They will need to issue you a certificate or provide access instructions.

whenToCallPro: If you need help installing client certificates, configuring smart card middleware, or troubleshooting certificate chain issues.

faq.q: What is a client certificate?
faq.a: A client certificate is a digital credential that proves your identity to a server, similar to how a server certificate proves the server's identity to you. It is like a digital ID card for accessing secure systems.

faq.q: Do I always need a client certificate to browse websites?
faq.a: No. The vast majority of websites only use server certificates. Client certificates are required only by specific high-security sites, typically in government, banking, or corporate environments.

title: Bad SSL Client Authentication Certificate
shortDescription: The client certificate presented to the server was rejected as invalid or expired.
fullDescription:
ERR_BAD_SSL_CLIENT_AUTH_CERT occurs when the browser presents a client certificate to a server that requires mutual TLS authentication, but the server rejects the certificate. The certificate may be expired, revoked, issued by an untrusted CA, or not meeting the server's requirements.

This error differs from ERR_SSL_CLIENT_AUTH_CERT_NEEDED in that a certificate was provided but rejected, rather than no certificate being available. The server examined the certificate and determined it is not acceptable.

This error is common when client certificates expire and need renewal, or when a system clock issue makes a valid certificate appear to be outside its validity period.

causes.cause: Client certificate has expired
Certificate issued by an authority the server does not trust
System clock is incorrect causing certificate to appear invalid
Certificate has been revoked
Wrong certificate type or key usage for the server's requirements

symptoms: Browser presents certificate dialog then fails after selection
Error occurs after selecting a client certificate from the list
Previously working certificate-authenticated sites now fail
Error message specifically mentions bad client auth cert

fixSteps.title: Check certificate expiration
fixSteps.instruction: Open Internet Options > Content > Certificates > Personal. Select your certificate and click 'View'. Check the 'Valid from' and 'Valid to' dates. If expired, you need a new certificate.

fixSteps.title: Verify system clock
fixSteps.instruction: Right-click the taskbar clock > Adjust date/time. Ensure the date, time, and timezone are correct. An incorrect clock can make a valid certificate appear expired.

fixSteps.title: Try a different certificate
fixSteps.instruction: If you have multiple certificates, the browser may be selecting the wrong one. When the certificate dialog appears, try selecting a different certificate.

fixSteps.title: Request a new certificate
fixSteps.instruction: Contact your organization's IT department or certificate issuer to request a new client certificate. Provide your old certificate's serial number if possible.

whenToCallPro: If you cannot determine why the certificate is being rejected, your IT department or the certificate issuer can check the server logs for the specific rejection reason.

faq.q: How do I renew an expired client certificate?
faq.a: Contact the organization that issued your certificate. They will typically verify your identity and issue a new certificate file or smart card. The process varies by organization.

faq.q: Can I use the same client certificate across different devices?
faq.a: Usually yes, if the certificate is exportable. Export it as a .pfx file from one device and import it on the other. Some organizations may restrict certificate export for security.

title: Network Access Denied
shortDescription: The browser's network access was blocked by a firewall, security software, or system policy.
fullDescription:
ERR_NETWORK_ACCESS_DENIED occurs when the browser's network requests are blocked at the system level, typically by a firewall, antivirus software, or group policy. Unlike server-side errors, this means the request never leaves your computer — something on your system is preventing the browser from making network connections.

This error can affect all websites or specific ones depending on how the blocking rules are configured. Antivirus software with web filtering features, Windows Firewall with specific outbound rules, or parental control software can all cause this error.

In corporate environments, Group Policy settings can restrict which applications can access the network, and these settings cannot be overridden by the user.

causes.cause: Antivirus or firewall blocking the browser's network access
Windows Firewall outbound rule blocking the browser
Parental control software restricting internet access
Group Policy restricting application network access
Third-party security software with web filtering

symptoms: All websites fail to load in the affected browser
Other browsers may work if not blocked
Other internet applications like email clients work normally
Error appears instantly without any loading delay

fixSteps.title: Temporarily disable antivirus/firewall
fixSteps.instruction: Temporarily disable your antivirus software and Windows Firewall. If sites load, the security software is blocking the browser. Re-enable and add browser as an allowed application.

fixSteps.title: Check Windows Firewall rules
fixSteps.instruction: Open Windows Security > Firewall & network protection > Advanced settings. Check both Inbound and Outbound Rules for any rules blocking your browser (chrome.exe, msedge.exe, firefox.exe).

fixSteps.title: Add browser to firewall exceptions
fixSteps.instruction: In Windows Firewall Advanced settings, click 'Outbound Rules' > 'New Rule' > 'Program'. Browse to your browser's executable and select 'Allow the connection'.

fixSteps.title: Check for parental controls
fixSteps.instruction: If parental controls or family safety features are active, they may be blocking internet access. Check Windows Settings > Accounts > Family & other users, or check third-party parental control software.

whenToCallPro: If the restriction is enforced by Group Policy on a domain-joined computer, only a domain administrator can modify the policy.

faq.q: Why can some apps access the internet but not my browser?
faq.a: Firewall rules can be configured per-application. Your browser may be specifically blocked while other applications are allowed. Check the firewall's application rules.

faq.q: Can Group Policy block my browser?
faq.a: Yes. In corporate environments, administrators can use Group Policy to block specific applications from accessing the network. These settings require administrator credentials to change.

title: Cleartext HTTP Not Permitted
shortDescription: The browser or system policy blocked an unencrypted HTTP request, requiring HTTPS instead.
fullDescription:
ERR_CLEARTEXT_NOT_PERMITTED occurs when the browser blocks an HTTP request because a security policy requires all traffic to use HTTPS encryption. This can be triggered by HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) preload lists, browser security settings, or enterprise policies that mandate encrypted connections.

As the web moves toward universal HTTPS, browsers are increasingly strict about unencrypted connections. Some browsers now default to HTTPS for all navigation and block fallback to HTTP unless explicitly allowed.

This error is most commonly seen when trying to access older websites that do not support HTTPS, or when the browser's HTTPS-Only mode or HTTPS-First mode is enabled.

causes.cause: Browser HTTPS-Only or HTTPS-First mode is enabled
Website is on the HSTS preload list requiring HTTPS
Enterprise policy mandating encrypted connections
Content Security Policy upgrade-insecure-requests directive

symptoms: HTTP URLs fail to load while HTTPS versions work
Browser shows security warning about unencrypted connection
Links to http:// URLs are automatically upgraded to https://
Old websites without HTTPS support become inaccessible

fixSteps.title: Try the HTTPS version
fixSteps.instruction: Change 'http://' to 'https://' in the URL. Many sites support both but this error only blocks the unencrypted version.

fixSteps.title: Disable HTTPS-Only mode
fixSteps.instruction: In Chrome: Settings > Privacy and security > Security > scroll to 'Always use secure connections' and turn it off. In Firefox: Settings > Privacy & Security > HTTPS-Only Mode > select 'Don't enable'.

fixSteps.title: Clear HSTS data for the site
fixSteps.instruction: In Chrome, go to chrome://net-internals/#hsts. Enter the domain in 'Delete domain security policies' and click Delete. This removes HSTS enforcement for that specific site.

fixSteps.title: Add site exception
fixSteps.instruction: If you need to access specific HTTP-only sites regularly, some browsers allow adding exceptions. In Firefox, HTTPS-Only mode can be disabled per-site using the padlock icon.

whenToCallPro: If the restriction is enforced by enterprise policy and you need to access an HTTP-only internal site, contact your IT department.

faq.q: Why is HTTP blocked?
