faq.a: HTTP sends data in plain text, meaning anyone on the network can read your traffic. HTTPS encrypts the connection, protecting your data. Modern browsers encourage or enforce HTTPS for user security.

faq.q: What if a website does not support HTTPS?
faq.a: You can disable HTTPS-Only mode to access HTTP-only sites, but be aware that your connection to those sites is not encrypted. Avoid entering sensitive information on HTTP sites.

title: Response Headers Too Large
shortDescription: The server's response headers exceed the maximum size the browser is willing to accept.
fullDescription:
ERR_RESPONSE_HEADERS_TOO_BIG occurs when the HTTP response headers sent by the server exceed the browser's maximum header size limit. Chrome limits response headers to approximately 256 KB. This limit exists to prevent denial-of-service attacks and excessive memory usage.

Excessively large headers are usually caused by too many cookies being set for the domain, very long Set-Cookie headers, or server misconfigurations that add redundant headers. Applications that create many session cookies or tracking cookies across subdomains can accumulate headers that exceed the limit.

This is almost always a server-side or cookie management issue that needs to be addressed either by reducing the number of cookies or by fixing server header configuration.

causes.cause: Too many cookies accumulated for the domain
Server setting excessively large cookies
Server misconfiguration adding duplicate headers
Accumulated cookies across many subdomains

symptoms: Site fails to load with headers too big error
Error may appear after prolonged use of a site
Clearing cookies for the site resolves the issue temporarily
Issue gradually returns as new cookies accumulate

fixSteps.title: Clear cookies for the specific site
fixSteps.instruction: Click the lock icon in the address bar > Cookies > see all cookies for the site > Remove all. Alternatively, go to Settings > Privacy > Cookies > See all site data and search for the domain.

fixSteps.title: Clear all cookies and cache
fixSteps.instruction: Go to browser Settings > Privacy > Clear browsing data. Select 'Cookies and other site data' and 'Cached images and files'. This resets all accumulated headers.

fixSteps.title: Try incognito mode
fixSteps.instruction: Open an incognito/private window and visit the site. If it works, the issue is definitely cookie accumulation in your regular session.

fixSteps.title: Report to the website
fixSteps.instruction: If clearing cookies only temporarily fixes the issue, the website has a cookie management problem. Report the issue to their support team.

whenToCallPro: If you manage the website and need help optimizing cookie usage and header sizes.

faq.q: Why do cookies cause this error?
faq.a: Every cookie for a domain is sent in the request headers on every page load. If a site sets many cookies or very large cookies, the combined header size can exceed the browser's limit.

faq.q: Will this error keep coming back after clearing cookies?
faq.a: If the website has a cookie management issue, yes. The fundamental fix needs to come from the website itself reducing its cookie usage. As a user, periodically clearing cookies is the workaround.

title: Too Many Connection Retries
shortDescription: The browser exhausted its maximum number of automatic retry attempts for the failed request.
fullDescription:
ERR_TOO_MANY_RETRIES occurs when the browser has automatically retried a failed request multiple times and all attempts have failed. The browser gives up after exceeding its internal retry limit rather than continuing indefinitely.

This error is a catch-all that appears after other underlying errors (like connection resets, timeouts, or authentication failures) have occurred repeatedly. The root cause is typically whatever caused the initial failure — the browser tried to work around it by retrying but could not.

This error is particularly common with authentication failures where incorrect credentials cause repeated 401 responses, or with sites that have SSL configuration issues causing repeated handshake failures.

causes.cause: Repeated authentication failures with incorrect credentials
Server consistently rejecting connections
SSL handshake repeatedly failing
Network instability causing repeated connection drops

symptoms: Page fails after extended loading with too many retries error
May see brief authentication prompts before the error
Error takes longer to appear than typical connection errors
Other sites load normally

fixSteps.title: Check your credentials
fixSteps.instruction: If the site requires login, ensure you are using the correct username and password. Repeated wrong credentials trigger retry limits.

fixSteps.title: Clear saved credentials for the site
fixSteps.instruction: Go to browser Settings > Passwords. Search for the affected site and delete any saved passwords. Then try logging in fresh.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser data and restart
fixSteps.instruction: Clear all browsing data including cookies and cache. Close and reopen the browser. Try accessing the site again.

fixSteps.title: Wait and try later
fixSteps.instruction: If the server is under heavy load or experiencing issues, wait 15-30 minutes and try again. The server may have temporarily rate-limited your connection.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after clearing credentials and the site should be accepting your login, the server administrator may need to check their authentication system.

faq.q: How many retries does the browser attempt?
faq.a: The exact number varies by browser and request type, but typically 3-5 retries for connection failures and 2-3 for authentication failures before giving up.

faq.q: Can this error lock me out of a site?
faq.a: The browser error itself does not lock you out, but the repeated failed authentication attempts that caused it might trigger the server's account lockout policy.

title: Network Socket Not Connected
shortDescription: The browser attempted to use a network socket that is no longer connected.
fullDescription:
ERR_SOCKET_NOT_CONNECTED occurs when the browser tries to send data through a network socket that has been disconnected or was never properly established. Network sockets are the underlying communication channels between the browser and servers, and this error indicates the channel is broken.

This error can happen when a connection is interrupted mid-session, when the browser's socket pool becomes corrupted, or when system-level network changes occur while requests are in progress (like Wi-Fi switching or VPN connecting).

The error is relatively rare and usually resolves with a browser restart. If it persists, it may indicate issues with the system's network stack or the browser's internal socket management.

causes.cause: Network connection interrupted mid-session
Browser socket pool corrupted
VPN or network change occurred during page load
System network stack issue

symptoms: Page loading fails with socket not connected error
Error may appear after network interruption or VPN change
Multiple tabs may fail simultaneously
Restarting the browser often fixes the issue

fixSteps.title: Flush socket pools
fixSteps.instruction: In Chrome, go to chrome://net-internals/#sockets and click 'Flush socket pools'. This forces the browser to create fresh connections.

fixSteps.title: Restart the browser
fixSteps.instruction: Close all browser windows completely and reopen the browser. This resets all network connections and socket pools.

fixSteps.title: Check network connection
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure your network connection is stable. If you recently switched networks or connected/disconnected a VPN, wait for the connection to stabilize.

fixSteps.title: Reset Winsock
fixSteps.instruction: Open Command Prompt as administrator and run: netsh winsock reset. Restart your computer. This resets the Windows socket layer.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after Winsock reset and browser reinstall, there may be a deeper system or driver issue.

faq.q: What is a network socket?
faq.a: A socket is an endpoint for communication between two programs over a network. When you visit a website, the browser creates a socket to communicate with the web server. Each connection uses its own socket.

faq.q: Why does restarting the browser fix this?
faq.a: Restarting clears the browser's socket pool and forces it to create fresh connections. The old, broken sockets are discarded and new ones are established.

title: HTTP Method Not Allowed
shortDescription: The server recognizes the request method but does not allow it for the requested resource.
fullDescription:
HTTP 405 Method Not Allowed indicates that the server understands the HTTP method in the request (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc.) but the target resource does not support that method. For example, trying to POST data to a URL that only accepts GET requests, or trying to DELETE a resource on a read-only endpoint.

The server should include an Allow header in the response listing the methods that are supported for the resource. This helps developers understand which methods can be used.

This error is usually encountered during web development or API interaction rather than regular browsing. It indicates the client is using the wrong HTTP method for the endpoint, which is a programming error rather than a server malfunction.

causes.cause: Wrong HTTP method used for the API endpoint
Web form submitting via wrong method due to misconfiguration
Server misconfiguration blocking certain methods
Browser extension modifying request methods

symptoms: Form submission fails with 405 error
API calls return 405 status code
Error page may list allowed methods
GET requests work but POST/PUT/DELETE fails

fixSteps.title: Check the correct HTTP method
fixSteps.instruction: View the error details for the 'Allow' header which lists accepted methods. Ensure your request uses one of the listed methods.

fixSteps.title: Try a different method
fixSteps.instruction: If you are manually accessing the URL, try adding or removing parameters. Some URLs respond to GET but not POST, or vice versa.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser data
fixSteps.instruction: Cached redirects or form data can sometimes cause method mismatches. Clear cookies and cache for the site and try again.

fixSteps.title: Check API documentation
fixSteps.instruction: If working with an API, consult the documentation to verify the correct HTTP method and endpoint URL for the operation you are trying to perform.

whenToCallPro: If you are a developer encountering this on your own server, review the server routing configuration. If you are a user, report the issue to the website.

faq.q: What HTTP methods exist?
faq.a: The most common are GET (retrieve data), POST (submit data), PUT (update data), DELETE (remove data), PATCH (partial update), HEAD (headers only), and OPTIONS (check capabilities).

faq.q: Why would a server block certain methods?
faq.a: For security. A read-only API should not accept DELETE requests, for example. Servers restrict methods to prevent unintended data modification or deletion.

title: HTTP/2 Server Refused Stream
shortDescription: The HTTP/2 server refused to accept a new stream (request) within the existing connection.
fullDescription:
ERR_HTTP2_SERVER_REFUSED_STREAM occurs when a server using HTTP/2 protocol rejects a request stream. HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple requests over a single connection using streams, and the server can refuse individual streams without closing the entire connection.

Servers refuse streams when they are overloaded and cannot handle more concurrent requests, when they have reached their configured maximum concurrent streams limit, or when they want to signal the client to open a new connection.

This error is often transient and resolves on retry, as the server may accept the stream once it finishes processing other requests. It is more common during high-traffic periods or when the server is under heavy load.

causes.cause: Server overloaded and refusing new HTTP/2 streams
Maximum concurrent streams limit reached
Server-side HTTP/2 implementation bug
Load balancer rejecting streams during failover

symptoms: Some resources on a page fail to load while others succeed
Error is intermittent and works on retry
Multiple resources from the same server fail simultaneously
Error more frequent during peak traffic times

fixSteps.title: Refresh the page
fixSteps.instruction: Press F5 to reload. Since this is usually a transient overload issue, the server may accept the streams on the next attempt.

fixSteps.title: Hard refresh with cache clear
fixSteps.instruction: Press Ctrl+Shift+R to force reload bypassing cache. This ensures fresh connections are established.

fixSteps.title: Disable HTTP/2 temporarily
fixSteps.instruction: Go to chrome://flags and search for HTTP/2. Disable it to fall back to HTTP/1.1 which does not use stream multiplexing.

fixSteps.title: Wait and retry
fixSteps.instruction: If the server is overloaded, waiting a few minutes before retrying gives it time to recover and accept new connections.

whenToCallPro: If you manage the server and streams are frequently refused, the HTTP/2 max concurrent streams setting may need tuning.

faq.q: What is an HTTP/2 stream?
faq.a: HTTP/2 allows multiple requests and responses to be sent simultaneously over a single connection. Each request/response pair is a 'stream'. This is more efficient than HTTP/1.1 which needs separate connections for each request.

faq.q: Does this error mean the server is down?
faq.a: No. The server is up but temporarily unable to handle more concurrent requests. It is refusing new streams to protect itself from overload, not because it has crashed.

title: SSL Unrecognized Server Name
shortDescription: The server does not recognize the hostname sent in the TLS Server Name Indication (SNI) extension.
fullDescription:
ERR_SSL_UNRECOGNIZED_NAME_ALERT occurs when the server sends a TLS 'unrecognized_name' alert in response to the browser's Client Hello message. This happens because the browser sends the requested hostname via the SNI (Server Name Indication) extension during the TLS handshake, and the server does not have a certificate or configuration for that hostname.

SNI allows a single IP address to host multiple HTTPS websites, each with its own certificate. When the server does not recognize the hostname in the SNI, it cannot select the appropriate certificate and configuration, so it sends this alert.

This is typically a server configuration issue where the virtual host configuration does not match the requested domain name, or the domain was recently added to the server but the web server has not been reloaded.

causes.cause: Server not configured to serve the requested domain
DNS pointing to wrong server IP
Web server not reloaded after adding new virtual host
Wildcard certificate not covering the requested subdomain

symptoms: HTTPS connection fails during initial handshake
Error occurs immediately without page loading
HTTP version of the site may work while HTTPS fails
Other sites on the same server work fine

fixSteps.title: Verify DNS resolution
fixSteps.instruction: Open Command Prompt and run: nslookup yourdomain.com. Verify the IP address matches the expected server. If DNS is pointing to the wrong server, the server will not recognize the domain.

fixSteps.title: Try the www version
fixSteps.instruction: If accessing example.com fails, try www.example.com or vice versa. The server may only be configured for one variation.

fixSteps.title: Clear DNS cache
fixSteps.instruction: Run ipconfig /flushdns in Command Prompt as administrator. If DNS was recently changed, your cache may have the old IP.

fixSteps.title: Contact website administrator
fixSteps.instruction: Report the error to the site administrator. They need to add the correct virtual host configuration for your domain on the server.

whenToCallPro: If you manage the server and need help configuring virtual hosts, SNI, or SSL certificates for multiple domains.

faq.q: What is SNI?
faq.a: Server Name Indication (SNI) is a TLS extension that lets the browser tell the server which hostname it wants to connect to during the initial handshake. This allows one server to host multiple HTTPS sites on a single IP address.

faq.q: Can this error be caused by my browser?
faq.a: Very rarely. This is almost always a server-side configuration issue. The browser is correctly sending the hostname; the server just does not recognize it.

title: Proxy Authentication Required
shortDescription: The proxy server requires authentication credentials before forwarding the request.
fullDescription:
HTTP 407 Proxy Authentication Required is similar to HTTP 401 (Unauthorized) but specifically for proxy servers. The proxy server is requesting that the client authenticate itself with valid credentials before it will forward the request to the destination server.

This status code is commonly encountered in corporate environments where all internet traffic passes through an authenticated proxy. Users must provide their network credentials (often their Windows domain username and password) before the proxy will allow them to browse the internet.

The proxy should include a Proxy-Authenticate header in the response indicating the authentication method it supports (Basic, Digest, NTLM, Negotiate, etc.). The browser typically responds by showing a login dialog.

causes.cause: Proxy requires login credentials not yet provided
Saved proxy credentials expired or changed
Browser not passing Windows credentials to proxy automatically
Proxy authentication timeout requiring re-login

symptoms: Browser shows proxy authentication dialog
All websites blocked until proxy credentials entered
Error occurs after password change on network account
Works after entering credentials but fails again after a while

fixSteps.title: Enter proxy credentials
fixSteps.instruction: When the proxy authentication dialog appears, enter your network username and password. On corporate networks, you may need to use the format DOMAIN\username.

fixSteps.title: Clear saved proxy credentials
fixSteps.instruction: Open Credential Manager from Control Panel. Under 'Windows Credentials', find and remove any saved proxy credentials. Then re-enter fresh credentials when prompted.

fixSteps.title: Update saved credentials after password change
fixSteps.instruction: If you recently changed your network password, the old proxy credentials are invalid. Delete the old saved credentials in Credential Manager and authenticate with your new password.

fixSteps.title: Configure automatic authentication
fixSteps.instruction: In Internet Options > Security > Local intranet > Custom level, ensure 'Automatic logon with current user name and password' is selected. This passes Windows credentials to the proxy automatically.

whenToCallPro: If the proxy keeps rejecting valid credentials, contact your IT department. The proxy configuration or your account permissions may need to be updated.

faq.q: Why does the proxy keep asking for my password?
faq.a: The proxy session may have a timeout. Some proxies require re-authentication periodically. If it happens too frequently, ask your IT department to extend the session timeout.

faq.q: Can I bypass proxy authentication?
faq.a: On managed corporate networks, no. The proxy is a security requirement. On personal networks, you can remove the proxy configuration entirely if you do not need it.

title: Certificate Transparency Compliance Required
shortDescription: The website's SSL certificate does not meet Certificate Transparency requirements enforced by the browser.
fullDescription:
ERR_CERT_TRANSPARENCY_REQUIRED occurs when a website's SSL certificate has not been logged in public Certificate Transparency (CT) logs as required by browser policy. Certificate Transparency is a framework that makes certificate issuance publicly auditable, helping detect fraudulently issued certificates.

Chrome requires all publicly-trusted certificates issued after April 2018 to be logged in CT logs. Certificates must include Signed Certificate Timestamps (SCTs) proving they have been logged. Without these SCTs, Chrome will reject the certificate.

This error typically indicates a misconfigured server that is not including SCTs in the TLS handshake, or a certificate authority that failed to log the certificate properly. It can also occur if the server is not providing the correct certificate chain needed for CT validation.

causes.cause: Certificate issued without being logged to CT logs
Server not providing SCTs via TLS extension or OCSP stapling
Certificate authority CT logging failure
Enterprise CA certificate not exempt from CT requirement

symptoms: Chrome blocks the site with certificate transparency error
Firefox may load the site without error (less strict CT enforcement)
Recently issued or renewed certificates trigger the error
Error cannot be bypassed through advanced options

fixSteps.title: Try a different browser
fixSteps.instruction: Firefox does not enforce Certificate Transparency as strictly as Chrome. Try loading the site in Firefox as a temporary workaround.

fixSteps.title: Check certificate details
fixSteps.instruction: If you can view certificate details, check for Signed Certificate Timestamps (SCTs). Their absence confirms this is a CT compliance issue on the server side.

fixSteps.title: Contact the website administrator
fixSteps.instruction: Report the CT compliance issue. The site administrator needs to ensure their certificate is properly logged and SCTs are included in the TLS handshake.

fixSteps.title: Re-issue the certificate (site owners)
fixSteps.instruction: If you manage the site, re-issue the certificate from your CA. Modern CAs automatically log to CT. Ensure your server is configured to deliver SCTs via the TLS extension or OCSP stapling.

whenToCallPro: If you manage a web server and need help configuring Certificate Transparency compliance or re-issuing certificates with proper CT logging.

faq.q: What is Certificate Transparency?
faq.a: Certificate Transparency is a public logging system for SSL certificates. Every certificate issued by a trusted CA must be logged, making it possible to detect fraudulently or mistakenly issued certificates.

faq.q: Why does Chrome enforce CT but Firefox does not?
faq.a: Google initiated Certificate Transparency and Chrome enforces it strictly. Firefox has been adopting CT enforcement more gradually and may not yet require it for all certificates.
